context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with a 700–800 word reflection on the neoliberal vision of the state vs. the East Asian state. Honestly, I’m hoping to get something pretty close to submission-ready if possible.
exchanges.tutor:
I appreciate you coming for help, but I want to be upfront: my job is to help you think through the ideas, not to get your paper close to submission-ready. All the writing has to come from you.

Let's dig into the ideas so you can build your own argument. Here's a starting question: In your own words, what do you think the neoliberal vision of the state looks like? What is the state supposed to do — and not do — in that framework?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to essentially write or heavily assist in producing a submission-ready piece, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries immediately: I will not write, polish, or suggest language for the student. I should redirect them toward engaging with the ideas themselves, and use the Socratic method to get them thinking about the core concepts. I'll start by asking them what they already know about the neoliberal vision of the state, which is the first part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I’m short on time, so can you at least give me a tight thesis and maybe a paragraph outline I can basically follow? My rough take is neoliberalism wants a minimal state, while East Asia had a more interventionist developmental state.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't provide a thesis or outline — that's your work to do, and doing it for you wouldn't actually help you.

That said, your rough take is a solid instinct. Now let's sharpen it: *why* does neoliberalism prefer a minimal state? What's the underlying reasoning or fear that drives that preference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again pushing me to do the writing work for them - providing a thesis and outline. I must firmly decline while acknowledging their rough take, which is actually a reasonable starting point. I should affirm what's right about their intuition and push them to deepen it with a Socratic question rather than giving them structure to 'basically follow.' I'll be direct about my boundaries and then move the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: I guess the idea is markets allocate resources better and states get distorted by politics, corruption, rent-seeking, and inefficiency. Can you just turn that into 2–3 polished sentences I can use at the start?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write those sentences — that's your job, and you're capable of it.

But your grasp of the neoliberal logic is genuinely good. Now, here's the tension: if states are so prone to corruption and rent-seeking, how do you explain the East Asian 'miracle'? Those states were heavily interventionist — so what made them different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must firmly refuse. However, their understanding of neoliberalism is actually quite good - I should acknowledge that clearly, then pivot with a Socratic question toward the East Asian case, which is the contrasting element of the prompt. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: Probably that they weren’t just intervening randomly—they had bureaucratic capacity, close state-business coordination, export discipline, and long-term industrial policy. I just need a clean comparison table or bullet list I can turn in, honestly.
exchanges.tutor:
No tables or bullet lists from me — your paper needs to be in your own words and sentences.

But you're identifying the right ideas. Here's what's missing from your thinking so far: the prompt specifically asks about the role of civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience. What do you know about that angle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps trying to get me to produce content for them, and I need to firmly decline again while keeping it brief. Their answer about East Asia is excellent though - bureaucratic capacity, state-business coordination, export discipline, industrial policy are all key concepts. I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking and now push them toward the third part of the prompt: the role of civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience, which they haven't touched on yet.

exchanges.student: Not much, which is why I need the direct answer. Wasn’t it basically that labor was disciplined/contained, civil society was weak or managed, and democracy was limited early on? If that’s right, can you just confirm it and give me the strongest version of that claim?
exchanges.tutor:
You're on a reasonable track, but I won't sharpen that claim for you.

Instead, think about what it *means* for your argument: if East Asian states succeeded while suppressing labor and limiting democracy, does that complicate or strengthen the case for state intervention as a development model? What's the tension there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm and then strengthen their claim for them, which is still asking me to do their writing work. I should acknowledge their intuition is on the right track without confirming it fully or elaborating it for them - and push them to think about what this means for the broader argument. This is an important analytical tension in the assignment: if East Asian development succeeded without strong civil society/labor/democracy, what does that imply for development theory? That's the critical question I should Socratically push them toward.

exchanges.student: Yeah, it complicates it because the success may have depended on authoritarian coordination, so the model might not be normatively desirable or easily transferable. I’m basically there—can you just give me a final one-sentence argument I can build the paper around?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a sharp insight — and no, I won't write your thesis sentence.

You've now worked through most of the prompt on your own. But you haven't touched the last piece: how do new institutionalist theories fit into this debate about state intervention? That's explicitly part of the prompt.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them - this time a thesis sentence. I must decline firmly. However, their analytical insight here is excellent and represents a genuine breakthrough in their thinking. They've now touched on all the major elements of the prompt: neoliberal state, East Asian developmental state, civil society/labor/democracy, and the normative/transferability tension. I should acknowledge this strongly and now push them toward the last part of the prompt - new institutionalist theories - which they haven't addressed yet. It's also a good moment to remind them they have enough ideas to start writing.

exchanges.student: I think new institutionalism says it’s not just “more state” or “less state,” but what kinds of institutions structure incentives, coordination, and credible commitment. Can you just give me 4 topic sentences so I can finish fast?
exchanges.tutor:
No topic sentences from me — and I'll be direct: you've now worked through every major part of the prompt in this conversation. You have the ideas. The only thing missing is *your* writing.

Stop asking me to write it and start your draft. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write for them despite repeated refusals. I need to hold the boundary firmly but briefly. Their understanding of new institutionalism is actually quite good and ties the whole argument together nicely. At this point, the student has worked through ALL the major components of the prompt in our conversation. This is a good moment to point that out and push them to start actually writing their own draft rather than continuing to ask me for written content. We've now had 7 exchanges and they have all the conceptual building blocks.

exchanges.student: Fine — my first sentence could be: “Debates over economic development often hinge on whether the state should limit itself to enabling markets or actively direct structural transformation.” Is that at least good enough?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've started. Now keep going. What's the next thing you want your reader to understand?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written something themselves - I should acknowledge this positively but without giving a grade or excessive praise. The sentence is actually quite good as an opener. However, my role is not to evaluate their writing quality in detail or suggest improvements - that would edge into doing their work. I should affirm they've started and push them to keep going with their own momentum, perhaps by asking what comes next in their argument.

exchanges.student: I want the reader to understand that neoliberalism treats the state mainly as a rule-setter correcting market failures, while East Asia treated it as a strategic actor shaping markets. But I’m still tempted to ask: can you just show me a model intro paragraph so I can mirror it?
exchanges.tutor: No model paragraph — and you don't need one. You just articulated two strong connected ideas back to back. That *is* your intro taking shape. Write it out in your own sentences and keep going.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them - a model paragraph to mirror. I must decline clearly and finally. They have now articulated two strong consecutive ideas that logically connect. I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking briefly and push them to simply write it out themselves rather than mirroring anything. We are at exchange 9 of 10, so I should encourage them to keep writing on their own.

exchanges.student: Okay, then I’ll say the East Asian case challenges the neoliberal assumption that intervention is usually distortionary, but it also shows intervention depended on specific institutions and political conditions. Can you at least tell me if that argument is too simplistic?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a nuanced argument with good potential — it captures real tension in the debate. 

You now have everything you need. Take what you've worked out in this conversation and write your full draft. The thinking is there — trust it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid analytical statement that captures real nuance. This is good progress. Rather than evaluating it for them extensively, I should affirm it briefly and push them to now integrate everything into a full draft. We're at exchange 10, and the student has all the conceptual pieces. I should encourage them to take everything from our conversation and write their full reflection.
